cataloging_subject.label: The Three Kings' Sons
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fifteenth-century Middle English prose translation of the anonymous French romance Les trois fils de rois, represented completely in the pinned XML. This intellectual work is distinct from its sole known surviving Middle English manuscript witness, the adjacent chronicle in Harley MS 326, Mrs Leon Kellner's working transcript, Leon Kellner's unpublished French collation, Furnivall's 1895 edition, the 1997 U-M encoding, and the checked-in repository artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Three Kings' Sons
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator; French source also anonymous
catalog_summary.editor: F. J. Furnivall (1895 source edition), from a working transcript made by Mrs Leon Kellner
catalog_summary.date: Late fifteenth century; attested by the sole surviving English witness dated c. 1475–c. 1485
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of translation unknown

titles.value: The Three Kings' Sons (Englisht from the French)
notes: This is Furnivall's expanded edition title, not evidence that Furnivall translated the work.

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English translator
notes: Neither the surviving witness nor the inspected scholarship identifies the translator.

agents.name: Mrs Leon Kellner
notes: Furnivall identifies her only as Leon Kellner's wife and does not state her personal name.

scope.description: Middle English translation of the anonymous French prose romance

notes: The closing formula says the book has been translated, but it does not name the translator or identify the physical French exemplar.

scope.description: The complete Middle English text in the 1895 source edition

notes: Editorial responsibility is a transmission-layer fact and does not imply intellectual authorship or translation.

notes: The transcript's present location and survival are unknown.

scope.description: Comparison of the English text with an unidentified French copy in Paris

assertion.notes: Furnivall states that the collation had been made for a projected Part II; he does not say that it altered the published Part I text.

date_statements.display: Late fifteenth century; no later than the surviving witness dated c. 1475–c. 1485
notes: The witness supplies a terminus ante quem, not a securely localized translation event.
The older circa-1500 date belongs to Ward's witness description and is not preferred over the current British Library range.

date_statements.display: c. 1475–c. 1485 in British Library Harley MS 326
1895 in F. J. Furnivall's EETS Extra Series 67 edition

scope.description: Digital representation date, not a date of medieval composition or translation

notes: This broad English context does not turn the witness's probable London production into a proven place of translation.
Caddick describes the surviving text as southern English; that linguistic observation remains distinct from a precise translation location.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The two-line Latin colophon is witness paratext; four English lines copied by a much later hand are annotation. Neither changes the primary language facet.

form_statements.description: One continuous prose romance organized in the witness as forty-five unnumbered chapters indicated by illuminated initials.

assertion.notes: The romance frames dynastic adventure and chivalric warfare as defense of Christian Sicily against a fictionalized Turkish power; the term describes this literary construction, not historical reportage.

subject_statements.label: Christian-Muslim conflict and conversion
notes: The text's representations of Turks, Islam, baptism, and conversion are late-medieval Christian literary constructions and should not be read as neutral ethnography.

subject_statements.label: Chivalry, tournaments, warfare, and military alliance

summaries.value: The princes Philip of France, David of Scotland, and Humphrey of England travel incognito to aid King Alfour of Sicily against a fictionalized Turkish invasion. Their military service, captivity, alliances, concealed identities, and love for Alfour's daughter Iolante lead to Christian victory, royal succession, a tournament for Iolante's hand, dynastic marriages, and conversions. The romance joins chivalric adventure and political counsel to strongly partisan late-medieval Christian representations of Islam and warfare.

The record describes the complete Middle English narrative at Harley MS 326, ff. 8r–123v, printed by Furnivall on pp. 1–207 and encoded as ThreeKSon:1–45. It excludes the preceding Chronicle from Rollo to Edward IV at ff. 1r–7r, Furnivall's forewords and p. 208 note, Thomas Austin's glossary on pp. 209–216, and all digital and physical transmission objects as intellectual parts.

Ward describes forty-five unnumbered manuscript chapters indicated by illuminated initials. Furnivall supplied running page glosses, and U-M reused those glosses as DIV1 headings. The chapter boundaries are therefore manuscript-supported while their displayed labels are modern editorial navigation. The repository XML has forty-five DIV1 elements, 115 work-text P elements outside note ancestry, 207 print-page markers across TEXT, and 231 folio-side milestones from 8v through 123v; f. 8r is identified in Furnivall's opening note rather than a milestone.

extent.description: One complete prose work in forty-five unnumbered manuscript chapters. The XML represents those chapters as ThreeKSon:1–45, with 115 prose paragraphs outside note ancestry, 207 print-page markers for Furnivall pp. 1–207, 231 explicit folio-side milestones spanning 8v–123v after the opening f. 8r statement, twenty-four NOTE1 elements, and six L elements: two Latin colophon lines in the work text and four later-hand English lines quoted in an editorial note. Encoding counts are not a canonical word or paragraph count.

content_parts.label: The Three Kings' Sons
content_parts.summary: The complete prose romance, from the upbringing of Philip and the appeal for aid to Sicily through the three princes' concealed service, the defeat of the Turkish forces, the Iolante tournament, royal marriages, conversions, and the closing account of the three kings' descendants.
notes: The forty-five chapter boundaries are summarized rather than repeated as forty-five intrinsic titled parts because their supplied labels are Furnivall's running glosses, not manuscript rubrics.

Furnivall's label Part I is bibliographic: the complete narrative is present. The projected Part II was to contain Kellner's introduction, vocabulary, syntax, and source study, not the missing end of the romance.

related_works.title: Les trois fils de rois
notes: ARLIMA describes the mid-fifteenth-century French prose romance as anonymous and says the David Aubert attribution appears false, although Aubert copied one witness.

The English translator's physical French exemplar and exact recension remain unidentified. BnF français 92 and other surviving French manuscripts are witnesses to the source work, not automatically the translator's exemplar.

evidence.citation: Target-scoped XML element, attribute, sequence, checksum, and Git-object inspection of ThreeKSon.xml.
evidence.locator: Complete file; BODY and direct TEXT children
evidence.summary: Verified 566,842 bytes, SHA-256 9912c653…, Git blob d6a7994f…, 45 sequential DIV1 nodes, 115 non-note P elements, 207 TEXT-wide PB elements numbered 1–207, 231 leaf milestones from 8v to 123v after the opening f. 8r note, 24 NOTE1, two Q1, and six L elements. The only work-text L elements are the two-line Latin colophon; four later-hand English lines occur inside an editorial note.

evidence.citation: ThreeKSon lineage evidence for Furnivall's complete 1895 source edition and inspected Robarts scan.
evidence.summary: Supports the full title, editor, EETS series and imprint, complete narrative pp. 1–207, omitted p. 208 note and pp. 209–216 glossary, Mrs Kellner transcript, Leon Kellner collation, editorial glosses, and Part I boundary.

evidence.citation: ThreeKSon lineage evidence for the current British Library Harley MS 326 record and dated image-access audit.
evidence.summary: Supports the sole surviving Middle English witness, exact ff. 8r–123v locus, current c. 1475–c. 1485 date, England probably London origin at witness level, physical description, twenty-two miniatures, forty-five unnumbered chapters, and current image unavailability.

evidence.citation: ThreeKSon lineage evidence for ARLIMA's English-work record.
evidence.summary: Supports English prose-romance classification, translation status, Harley MS 326 witness census, 1895 edition bibliography, ARLIMA 2070, and Wikidata Q123529940.

evidence.citation: ThreeKSon lineage evidence for ARLIMA's French source-work record.
evidence.summary: Supports the anonymous mid-fifteenth-century French prose romance, title variants, eight listed surviving French manuscripts, source-work identifiers, and the rejected or doubtful David Aubert authorship attribution.

evidence.citation: ThreeKSon lineage evidence for Ruth Helen Caddick's 2023 PhD thesis.
evidence.summary: Supports the late-fifteenth-century prose-romance classification, sole surviving Harley witness, c. 1475–c. 1485 date, southern English dialect description, anonymous English translator and French author, source-title variants, and doubt about Aubert authorship.

open_questions.question: When, where, and for whom was the Middle English translation made?
next_steps: Consult Henry Grinberg's complete 1975 filiation study and subsequent scholarship on the Anglo-Burgundian transmission.
Separate translation history from Harley 326's c. 1475–c. 1485 production in England, probably London.
Do not promote the surviving witness's southern English dialect or probable London origin into a proven translation location without textual evidence.

open_questions.question: Which French recension and physical copy supplied the English translator, and which Paris copy did Leon Kellner collate?
next_steps: Compare Grinberg 1975 with Giovanni Palumbo's 2001 edition and 2002 manuscript study.
Trace EETS archives for Mrs Kellner's transcript, Leon Kellner's collation, and any surviving shelfmark correspondence.
Keep the conceptual translation_of relation to Les trois fils de rois separate from any surviving French witness until physical identity is evidenced.

open_questions.question: Can all forty-five manuscript chapter boundaries and their relationship to Furnivall's running glosses be checked against a complete current color facsimile?
next_steps: Ask the British Library whether the unavailable legacy full-digital object can be restored.
Otherwise request the whole codex, not only ff. 8r–123v, and map each illuminated initial against the edition's forty-five headings.
Continue to label the boundaries as manuscript-supported and the displayed heading texts as editorial.

notes: Furnivall's Part I contains the complete narrative; the projected Part II was a scholarly introduction and language/source study and was never published.
Harley MS 326 is the sole known surviving Middle English manuscript witness, not the sole witness to the multilingual romance: ARLIMA lists eight surviving French manuscripts.
The current British Library c. 1475–c. 1485 witness date is preferred while Ward's older about-1500 description and Furnivall's title-page wording remain attributed historical assertions.

Internet Archive metadata assigns David Aubert as a creator, but current source-work evidence treats the French romance as anonymous and Aubert as a copyist of one French witness; the provider heading is not adopted as authorship.
No Oxford Text Archive identifier or transfer statement appears in the XML header or pinned CME history; a possible OTA intermediary remains unconfirmed in the lineage questions.
